title: Agent Memory Systems
objective: Frame memory as an engineering discipline for long-running agents.
coreClaim: Agent memory is a data system around the model, not a larger prompt.
practicalTakeaway: Design memory with storage, retrieval, policy, and observability.

title: Learning Goals
objective: Set expectations for taxonomy, mechanisms, tooling, and operations.
coreClaim: A useful memory deck must separate concepts, implementation paths, and production tradeoffs.
practicalTakeaway: Judge every memory option by the workload it supports.

title: Running Example
objective: Introduce the customer account agent used across the deck.
coreClaim: A customer account agent exposes the hard parts: identity, history, commitments, product facts, policy, and handoffs.
practicalTakeaway: Use a concrete workflow when evaluating memory abstractions.

title: Memory Is Not Chat History
objective: Separate transcript retention from agent memory.
coreClaim: Chat history is one input; memory is selected, structured, scoped, and governed context.
practicalTakeaway: Avoid calling every retained message memory.

title: Layer Map
coreClaim: Production memory combines runtime state, product truth, long-term memory, retrieval indexes, and governance.
practicalTakeaway: Assign one owner and one data contract to each layer.

title: Scopes Before Types
objective: Show that ownership boundaries determine correctness.
coreClaim: The same memory fact can be safe in a user scope and dangerous in a fleet scope.
practicalTakeaway: Model thread, user, account, organization, agent, and fleet scopes explicitly.

title: Memory Taxonomy
coreClaim: Working, session, episodic, semantic, procedural, and shared memory solve different jobs.
practicalTakeaway: Choose memory type from the decision the agent needs to make.

title: Working Memory
coreClaim: Working memory keeps task-relevant state close to the model without pretending it is permanent truth.
practicalTakeaway: Expire working memory aggressively.

title: Session Memory
coreClaim: Session memory preserves a single conversation or run, usually through message history and checkpointed state.
practicalTakeaway: Use sessions for continuity, not for durable knowledge.

title: Long-Term Memory
coreClaim: Long-term memory persists facts, episodes, and procedures across threads and must be scoped outside a single run.
practicalTakeaway: Give long-term memory lifecycle and correction semantics.

title: Memory Unit
coreClaim: A memory unit needs type, subject, scope, content, provenance, confidence, validity, policy, and indexes.
practicalTakeaway: Persist metadata with the remembered content.

title: Write Path
coreClaim: The write path transforms raw interaction into governed records through extraction, classification, policy, consolidation, and indexing.
practicalTakeaway: Evaluate writes separately from reads.

title: Hot Path Or Background
coreClaim: Hot-path writes improve immediate personalization but add latency; background consolidation reduces latency but can lag.
practicalTakeaway: Use hot-path writes only for memories needed on the next turn.

title: Extraction Rules
coreClaim: The hardest write-path decision is what not to remember.
practicalTakeaway: Reject transient, unverified, sensitive, and low-value candidates by default.

title: Consolidation
coreClaim: Consolidation turns a pile of memories into an evolving model of the user, account, and domain.
practicalTakeaway: Track superseded records instead of overwriting history blindly.

title: Indexing
coreClaim: One memory can need exact lookup, semantic search, graph traversal, recency ordering, and audit lookup.
practicalTakeaway: Persist records once, index them several ways.

title: Read Path
objective: Trace memory retrieval and prompt assembly.
coreClaim: The read path is query planning plus retrieval, ranking, policy filtering, and bounded context assembly.
practicalTakeaway: Log selected and rejected memory ids for debugging.

title: Retrieval Signals
coreClaim: Useful memory ranking blends relevance, recency, confidence, scope, source quality, and policy.
practicalTakeaway: Do not let vector score be the only arbiter.

title: Prompt Budget
coreClaim: A memory system earns value by deciding what to omit from the prompt.
practicalTakeaway: Budget context by memory type and decision risk.

title: Freshness And Contradictions
coreClaim: Memory should be revisable because user facts, accounts, policies, and preferences change.
practicalTakeaway: Model validity windows and correction workflows.

title: Storage Role Map
coreClaim: Databases add value by separating product truth, memory records, retrieval indexes, graph relations, caches, and audit logs.
practicalTakeaway: Do not collapse product truth and remembered context into one vague store.

title: MongoDB Framing
coreClaim: MongoDB frames agent memory around memory types, memory units, application modes, and unified storage/retrieval.
practicalTakeaway: Use document flexibility when memory shape evolves quickly.

title: Postgres And pgvector
coreClaim: Postgres with pgvector is attractive when memory belongs near relational identity, constraints, and transactional application data.
practicalTakeaway: Use relational constraints for identity and access boundaries.

title: Graph Memory
coreClaim: Graph memory becomes valuable when the question depends on relationships, time, entities, and multi-hop context.
practicalTakeaway: Use graph edges for relationships that must be inspectable.

title: LangChain And LangGraph
coreClaim: LangGraph handles thread state with checkpoints and long-term memory through stores and namespaces.
practicalTakeaway: Separate checkpoint resume from cross-thread memory.

title: Deep Agents And LangMem
objective: Explain file and tool-based memory in LangChain's newer stack.
coreClaim: Deep Agents and LangMem move memory toward scoped files, tools, and DB-backed stores rather than opaque transcript buffers.
practicalTakeaway: Plan production persistence even when demos use in-process stores.

title: Mastra Memory
coreClaim: Mastra combines message history, observational memory, working memory, semantic recall, processors, tracing, and storage providers.
practicalTakeaway: Use resource and thread identifiers as deliberate memory boundaries.

title: LlamaIndex Memory
coreClaim: LlamaIndex exposes memory as a configurable component with `put` and `get` behavior around agent execution.
practicalTakeaway: Use custom memory when retrieval policy matters more than default chat history.

title: OpenAI Agents SDK Sessions
coreClaim: OpenAI Agents SDK sessions maintain conversation history across runs and can be backed by custom storage.
practicalTakeaway: Treat sessions as conversation continuity, not full long-term memory.

title: Managed Memory Services
coreClaim: Dedicated services move extraction, retrieval, graphing, and management out of application code.
practicalTakeaway: Adopt services when memory quality and operations are central product requirements.

title: Mem0
coreClaim: Mem0 positions itself as a managed memory engine with user, agent, session, graph, and core operation workflows.
practicalTakeaway: Evaluate Mem0 on write quality, retrieval precision, deletion, and cost.

title: Framework And Service Map
coreClaim: Framework memory, app-owned memory, dedicated memory services, and governed shared memory optimize for different owners.
practicalTakeaway: Choose based on who owns the memory contract and operational risk.

title: Zep
coreClaim: Zep emphasizes adding and retrieving agent memory through a high-level API backed by graph search and custom context construction.
practicalTakeaway: Pair graph-derived long-term context with recent raw messages.

title: Letta
coreClaim: Letta treats state, memory blocks, messages, reasoning, tool calls, and tools as persisted agent state.
practicalTakeaway: Use Letta when transparent editable agent state is central.

title: Cognee And Memori
coreClaim: Cognee and Memori push memory toward graph/session recall and multi-agent memory processing.
practicalTakeaway: Validate their fit with your graph, MCP, and multi-agent requirements.

title: Emerging Layers
objective: Place Rippletide, Semvec, and Caura carefully.
coreClaim: Emerging layers are useful signals for direction, but their maturity and source depth vary.
practicalTakeaway: Borrow concepts before betting on a young runtime.

title: Privacy And Deletion
coreClaim: Memory creates retained data; retained data needs consent, access control, deletion, audit, and sensitivity handling.
practicalTakeaway: Design deletion before storing user memories.

title: Governance Loop
coreClaim: Governed memory moves through approval, retrieval, use, correction, expiry, deletion, and audit.
practicalTakeaway: Make every memory state explicit and inspectable.

title: Memory Evals
coreClaim: Memory evaluation must test both write quality and read usefulness under realistic tasks.
practicalTakeaway: Build evals around correction, contradiction, deletion, and tenant isolation.

title: Observability
coreClaim: A memory trace should show candidate extraction, write decisions, retrieved memories, omitted memories, and prompt assembly.
practicalTakeaway: Debug memory by inspecting dataflow, not only final answers.

title: Failure Modes
coreClaim: Memory failures include over-remembering, stale facts, poisoned memory, cross-tenant leakage, vector false positives, and unbounded cost.
practicalTakeaway: Turn each failure mode into a test.

title: Implementation Recipe
coreClaim: Start with product truth and session memory, then add scoped long-term memory, then managed or graph layers when evidence demands it.
practicalTakeaway: Avoid adopting a memory service before defining what counts as a memory.

title: Decision Matrix
coreClaim: The best memory strategy depends on ownership, retention, graph needs, governance needs, and framework coupling.
practicalTakeaway: Pick the smallest strategy that satisfies correctness and operations.

title: Code-Level Shape
coreClaim: A memory port should hide storage details but expose scope, provenance, policy, and trace ids.
practicalTakeaway: Own the interface even when a framework owns the implementation.

title: Recap
coreClaim: Good memory is typed, scoped, governed, evaluated, observable, and tied to product truth.
practicalTakeaway: Ask what the agent should remember, who can see it, and how it can be corrected.

title: References
coreClaim: The deck is grounded in official docs and a small number of contextual research sources.
practicalTakeaway: Use grouped references to continue researching by concept.
